speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Willa Cummings Carlson explores the views of American ridgers, what men and women talked about day to day, their attitudes about politics, differences between Northerners and Southerners, and prejudice towards Negroes. She reports a rumor of an early vigilante hanging, a mirage of Kendrick's Main Street ablaze on the sky, a fire that burned down the Carlson house, and typhoid fever that struck the family.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did you say? We can go down to the finale? Well I say, which will we go?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: There's going to be. If it's a climax, and I think it's a climax, and it's been building and building. Alright, now when the climax breaks, it goes over. We don't stop, things go, the wheels turn. Well alright, and anything could be comparing it to anything, any of life's crises, after the climax, there's a finale.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Now are we going to go down toward that finale? Or will we start building toward a new finale?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: That's going to take engineering.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well when you say we have the edifice, what is it? What do you mean by that? Well don't you think that what I mean is this?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: I, I don't know if you get the idea, but I think of anything that we build in the way of a skeleton government of any kind, it's been built hasn't it, by human hands. And after it's done, you know, many of the edifices with which we deal are not seen by the human, by the eye.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Don't you think that, that there's an edifice been built? Well is it built on solid ground, is it built on rock, or is it built on sand?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: And just as those that we have, all the signs, all the similes that we've given in the literature and everywhere else, well don't think we're not building them all the time ourselves.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And what are we doing? I used to try to teach my children that you're building all the time, now where are you building?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: Are you building solidly or, oh well, some of the kids, some of my own, you know some of them, you probably see which ones mostly.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, what do you know about it? And how can you tell?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: And well, you can do what you want to, and you can tear down and build up again. You have to build with repairs, and of course that's true in many cases. And they always think they're engineers of their own building, but they're not always. If they can interpret and see what's coming, go with it or against it, solidly, I think they're better off.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Have you read Late Planet Earth?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: No.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: Well, you've had, you say, a Jewish background, which interests me because the Jewish background, with the few Jews I've been privileged to talk with about the background, their background, ordinarily those that I know, they've had it and that's it, and they don't want to enter into anything.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: It's life?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: I think so.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: And history bears us out, all countries have come to those crossroads.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What do you think that this crossroads is? Is it a crossroads of moral fiber, ethical?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: Largely.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What is it?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: Largely. That's caused the downfall. There is a tremendous effort to get them back on the road of strict morality, and then a little part of it is looseness.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: I deplore the tearing down of, now I believe in constitutions, don't get me wrong. I'm liberal in a lot of views, but when it comes to living, I believe in law and order, much as I deplore the kind of laws we make and the decisions that we come to sometimes. But nevertheless, I believe in order, and at heart, I am a conservative, in a way. I don't believe in reckless waste of human energy or human power in any way.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: And as far as all this business of racial contention and wanting another, it's been terrible and still we had it. We should have known. And you look back at history, of course, our hindsight, the hindsight of all of us is so much better than our foresight, naturally. You've had that told to us many times, and I, for one, have accepted it because I can see the truth of it all around me. But now, we should know as we look back over history, and I think it is significant in our history. And I don't hear very many making anything of it, and I know I've mentioned it several times, and even historians say, well, that's one of the accidents, just an accident that naturally when things are going on, they'll come at the same time. But look what happened. Now, back in 1619 happens to be the year in which the House of Bridgeses, which was a stab at getting self -government and real democratic government of the people in Virginia, the new colony in the new world. And I speak of 1619, I'm talking about the new world of the United States, not because I belong to the United States altogether. Of course, that's why I'm looking at it naturally, because I happened by accident to have been born here.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Accident?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: No.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: I don't believe things happened by accident. And that was that year anyway it started, and all the world had turned their eyes on the colonies. And here was this colony, and we were going to try and experiment with self -government. And at that time, there were several patriots that had come in and they had made their speeches and made their stabs for this particular sort of thing. And then that same year was the year when the Dutch ship landed with their cargo Negroes, slaves that they had brought from Africa in Virginia. And there were these colonists, many of them, those that had gone up here to establish self -government, out there elbowing each other out to get the best slaves, buying the slaves and introducing them into Virginia. The very same year, in the very same colony, they were introduced. And it was a Dutch trading vessel that brought in, I have always pointed that out too to the English, who were so proud of their record. And it was a Dutch trading vessel that brought the Negroes, the first Negroes, into the colony of Virginia.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: There was also a little, a small attempt made to course some of the drinking that was going on there, because there was a lot of that. You forget that sometimes when talking about race, they were bringing up a lot of, you can be sure that licentiousness prevailed in some of those places.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I'd like to ask you to look at, now to look at the, on the ridge in the old days, what were the attitudes, this is very interesting to me, what were the attitudes that you remember towards race on the ridge?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: Well, toward race. I mean the Negro problem. You mean the Negro problem. Well, I'll tell you, I was a child and of course I didn't know the big things. I didn't know the big overall things. I hadn't philosophized. I hadn't taken into consideration the religious aspect that I learned to have later. I had to learn that. I didn't come born with it. I came born, no doubt, with my people were not even religious people. And of course they wondered where I got my ideas when I'd come home with them and many times I was shut up because they didn't want me talking about such things. But, so I didn't know too much when I was little. But I did know about the Negro problem. I was afraid of Negroes. Now I must have gotten that fear from people around me because no other place. My mother was from Missouri and she knew a lot of Negroes and the Negroes as she knew, she was perfectly at home with and talked about them. But she had the idea of all the white people. Now for instance, she trained me to always say Mr. and Mrs. and to this day that was good training and after I grew up even I realized it was good training because a lot of people thought that they said to me, well you must have had pretty good training because you always say Mr. and Mrs. to the older people and you're quite polite. And I was complimented for my politeness by some very rough talking people. People that never thought of saying anything but your first name. And I still have that notion that if the people are strange to me I'm one of the last ones to start calling them by their first name. That seems to me to be a little more familiar than you ought to be with strangers. But my mother's reason now, coming back to what the training would be, was that you called the Negroes, the servants, by their first names because after all they didn't have any other name. No, they weren't permitted. As I learned when I grew a lot older that they weren't permitted, they had their own marriage rights. A lot of them did. And they married in those old rough cabins. But they weren't given the right of sanctity and marriage. They weren't allowed to go to a church and be married.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: No sir.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: They weren't permitted anything. They weren't given any legal rights. And when it came right down to it, look at the Dred Scott decision that a Negro slave was property and should be treated as property. Hey, I had no more right than a horse or a cow.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that the attitudes on the Ridge were in the abstract? How did they come down to a guy like Joe Wells who lived in the area and was a Negro?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: Well, I'll tell you.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: I wondered about that too. But I knew a man, we know a man, and you probably run into him too. Old Elmer Wells. He still lives down at the Idaho Hotel. Well, that's where Joe Wells got his name. The Wells family lived back in North Carolina along with all these mixes that are here. The old mixes. The ones that came before them. I didn't know any of them, but I just know of the family. The mix family. And Elmer Wells practically worshipped at the feet of old Gub Mix and his wife. They called old Gub Mix and his wife here. He came here as a young man. And my husband knew him and worked with him before he died. Of course, he was a lot older than my husband. But he came here as a young man, was politically interested politically in one thing or another. Came from North Carolina. Well, Elmer Wells had known them back there, but they'd been poor compared to the mixes. But still some of the Wellses had wealth enough to have slaves. And they loved their slaves. They were very lovable people, some of those. But still they weren't recognized as people. Even in the Constitution, it took three -fifths. Three -fifths of the Negroes were counted as people. So you were only three -fifths people, a person, as a slave. Clear back to the Constitution. A lot of people don't know that now because not a lot of people don't know what the Constitution says. I think there are lots of people that never read the Constitution. I'm not very conversant with it now because I haven't read it in so long. But when I was in school, I was very much interested in government and I sure reread the Constitution. And I learned that we didn't have a lot of rights that we thought we had. But then we treated the Negro shamefully and always did.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And couldn't somebody see that that was a terrible thing, way to treat those people? Isn't it something terrible to think that our ancestors didn't know how to do? And that they could look upon human beings as three -fifths of people? Isn't that awful?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: It's ridiculous.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: And I've been talking about that ever since I learned it down in grade school. Did people...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was there actually towards Joe Wells, though?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: Well, Joe Wells was like so well by Elmer Wells. They came out here some way. That family of slaves followed the Wellses out here. And I don't know. I never did know that story because I didn't know them. I didn't live in that neighborhood. And not until long afterward did I know that he got his name from Elmer Wells's family. But Elmer worked for my husband for a while. He was old, but he could still grub brush. And my husband was athened for grubbing out brush by hand. And even though the tract of land that he cleared, it wouldn't ever produce enough to pay for the wages of clearing it. But that's the way my husband was. That's the kind of an economist he was. And he did not know how to manage money. And awful good fellow in many, many ways. But I'm sure Elmer Wells would have thought that because he was a good Democrat, though, for Elmer Wells. And Elmer Wells was a very good Democrat and he went around making an awful bad noise with some ugly words about the Republicans. That was his style of being a Democrat. But he liked Joe Wells. But still, Joe Wells was a Negro. He was not up to date with them. Now I understood that when the Wellses came down here, I don't know this for a fact. And I can't, and I'm not telling it as a fact, nor even suggesting it's a fact. Hearsay. But, yes, it was pure hearsay. But I did hear that he would be invited to eat with mixes, but he didn't eat at the same table. They always had another table for them. I don't know about that. But a fellow by the name of Thompson ran in Moscow Hotel and he said, well, old Joe Wells can sure eat here. But at that time they had a rule in the Moscow Hotel that no Negroes could stay overnight in the hotel. So a group of singers came in here quite a long time ago. And I was young and out of an American marriage and I had no influence and had no way of doing things. And my father wouldn't have thought of mixing in anything like that. He wouldn't have thought of it. He didn't think it was right. I don't mean to say that Papa thought it was right. He said there's no right to do that to those people. But lock them out of the hotel or refuse to feed them or give them housing. But people, there'd be people here around town, here and there, that would take them in for a night's lodging and feed them. And that's the way they did eat. Well, they went over to Pullman and Pullman wouldn't take them in either for a while. But Pullman, and their main hotel over there in Washington Hotel, admitted the Negro to the hotel before they did over here at the Moscow Hotel. Now at that time there was only the Moscow Hotel in question because it was the big hotel here. The only building that called itself a hotel. Anything else there would be boarding houses or lodging houses. But there were no other hotels at first. I can remember the time when that was the only hotel. And a lot of people thought that was right. Why the idea of a Negro thinking he could go there. And there were just lots and lots of people wouldn't think of letting a Negro sleep in their house. But evidently there were some people who did because they got places to stay. Oh yes, there were some who did. They were divided. Now the people on American Ridge even who were good people, and I think that for the most part they would have wanted to see a Negro get justice.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: I think.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: But we had the South Methodist Church and the North Methodist.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: And when I was a little girl I said, why is it the Emmy Church North is up here, or the Emmy Church South is up here on the north end of the ridge?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: And I could ask that.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: Oh nobody, why do you care?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: Nobody paid any attention to me among the younger people of course. And the older people didn't want to talk about it. I'm sure that was the reason they turned me off. But I did ask that because it was a question to me, but my question wasn't on Negro subjects.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: It was on why was a South Methodist Church on the north end of the ridge? And why was a South Methodist Church, or why was a North Methodist Church down on the south end of the ridge?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: Two Methodist churches. And the people that went to them. Now here and there was a family that would always go to the other end of the ridge to go to church because it was the right kind of church. Well finally, I suppose it was Ms. Roberts that told me, I don't want to say so, but I think so. That, well, it was the people down on the south end of the ridge were all Northerners. So they had a North Methodist Church. And on the upper end of the ridge there were quite a few people that had come from the south, or southern sympathizers, or from Kansas where they had southern sympathizers. So there were southern sympathizers and they named it the South Methodist Church. But they brought it along with them from the east where they'd come from because they were all from the east. Now this was not completely true because the lines weren't that clearly defined. There were people that went to the church because it happened to be the church where they were set up and they realized that they didn't have any Negroes out here so they had no Negro problem. But that was the reason that it was separated in the first place. It was a North and South Methodist Church. And so I found out.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: To what extent do you think that the differences between Northerners and Southern parts of the country did play a part in the lives of the people on the ridge?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: Well it played a part, not a big part. But they didn't talk, well it was understood that this fellow, they say that he fought in the Confederate army, they'd say. Well he was an old Confederate soldier. Well they nodded their head and looked around. They didn't shun him but they didn't trust him completely either. There was that feeling, that feeling of distrust about the Confederates.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: On the part of the Northerners.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: On the part of the Northerners of course. And vice versa. I don't know about that. I didn't have to be around enough Southern people to hear them say. And there was a Negro came to town once. I'm not, I don't mention this and I don't tell it and I wouldn't write down anything about it because I do not know at this late date, I don't know what the real facts were. And I'm just indebted to the people who were around me for, and they had only hearsay. Those were people that weren't in town. This happened in Troy. And of course we lived out in the country and I heard scraps of conversation. And I heard everything because I listened. And this man, a Negro came to town. I don't know what he did. I don't know what he was. I think I knew at the time what he was there for. But just a common, poor, working man.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: And I was very, very young. I must have been down, oh I was down in the lower grades. I, eight or nine at the most was what I was. Because that's been a long time. And there was a widow woman who had come into the country. But I don't remember when she came so she would come before I ever went to school or anything. Mrs. Clem, she kept house for her brother in the country. And she had two sons. And they were rough ones I guess, both of them, the drinkers and so on. But anyway, Rob and Walter. Well I learned to know Walter Clem real well. And he reformed his drinking and he has a family of seven children I think. They are strung out over American Ridge. Of course they wouldn't know anything about their father's dealings. And all these things happened before Walter ever had the family. But I learned to know Walter Clem real well. And liked him. He was a likable fellow. And he had this brother Bob who was not so likable I'm told. And I never knew him. I knew him when I saw him. And he had a wife. Walter wasn't married at that time. He was much, much younger. He had a wife and a daughter. And the daughter was just practically a baby when this happened. But there was a cafe in town and it was run by, they said, a southerner. And of course he wasn't, he was a southerner that knew Negroes and they'd been around him and wasn't one that hated the Negro. Not by any means. And he let him eat at his cafe. So he ate at this man's cafe. And I don't know what about his lodging. There was a question about that. Well he and this Bob Clem got into a fight one night. A drunken fight. Raw. In the cafe. And the Negro was killed.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: Now wait a minute. I've heard that a Negro was killed in Troy before.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: Wait a minute.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was the Negro killed?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: Or Bob Clem was killed I think. Maybe they were both killed. I think Bob Clem died that way. So then they were going to hang a Negro. That was the talk.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: Lynching. Lynching.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: And there was that talk all around. And I remember that there were, I've heard since, that there were cool heads that stopped a lynching party. But maybe they both died.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: But I remember them saying, well it was good riddance of bad rubbish if the Negro got killed. Everybody was in favor of getting him killed. And he hadn't done a thing. I heard that. Hadn't done a thing wrong. Oh and they go down there and I suppose he was karousing. Same as Clem was. You see I don't know the particulars of that. I don't know the particulars of that. And so I can't tell them at all. But I do know that there was this terrible fight between Bob Clem and the Negro. Now whether they were both killed or one was killed and the other wasn't. But I think Bob Clem was killed because everybody sympathized with his widow. I'm not so sure she was so deserving of sympathy. But there was a lot of sympathy expended on medical. You don't have any idea what eventually happened to that Negro. Whether he was in himself died or tried. He wasn't lynched. No he wasn't lynched and I'm sure he wasn't tried. I'm pretty sure he either was killed or died from that. I'm pretty sure of that. You know I haven't thought of that for many years. It just triggers it from thinking about his relations. Thinking about those times. Yes it did. And so I haven't thought of that for a long time. I'm telling you my husband's father could have told you all about it because he was oh my he was incensed by it and by the treatment of the Negro. But he'd come from Sweden and hated slavery.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: And my father -in -law was certainly not moderate about anything.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why did he hate slavery so much?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: Because he didn't believe in it.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: Was that...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it part of his religious upbringing too? In Sweden?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: Yes.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: He had that upbringing. I know that. And so of course it was partly that.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: But oh no.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: He hated that. And yet, I remember him saying one time, I shouldn't be telling you, but he considered himself an authority on religion and politics. And he believed that he booked no interference from his family. And of course I didn't know any better than to take a stand against him a time or two. But I learned to keep still and just let him go and let him believe what he wanted to believe. But I do remember him saying well the Negroes couldn't go any farther because the Bible prophesies. And I said well that's... I didn't know that you had the Mormon idea. That's the Mormon idea too. And I... that was on a hearsay with me. I didn't know that the Mormons had it. I hadn't studied Mormonism or anything at that time. But they do have a pretty black image of the Negro in there. As far as that's concerned. But then I read the Bible and get something entirely different to my father -in -law. And I believe in the Bible. I have great faith in it. But then there are large parts of it that I admit I don't understand. So that I wouldn't think of arguing with them. I read them and take it into consideration and I ask them questions and is that what you believe. But as far as me starting to argue and saying no, no, no and all that I don't do that on these people who have interpreted it. But of course you know it does say in there that Ham, the sons of Ham shall be the hearers of wood and the drawers of water. In the camps of Shem of course. Shems and Jacob too as far as that was concerned. But Shem. And Shem has of course they say will always make a slave of the Negro. And that it's, well of course if that were absolutely true then Shem is no more to blame than anybody else. If it's a curse on Shem he's got to put up with this hearer of wood and drawers of water. You know. Now that's a place to which these people who are arguing that don't want to go. It doesn't put, the Bible certainly doesn't put Shem in the camp to blame for it. It was leveled at them according to the Bible. Now that's quite a mystery to me. And it's been a mystery. A lot of other things are mysteries to me. I believe that there is that element of absolute truth in them. But that we have it I don't know about. And I certainly didn't know my father knew I had it either.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: But.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: Was there. No there weren't. Paul's father lived on of course went on birthdays. He didn't on American News. And most people were beneath his favor of even talking with him because he didn't think they knew much. And there were a lot of ignorant people. Early settlers as far as that was concerned. But I'm not so sure they were as ignorant as you thought they were. But the Swedes out on Burnt Ridge were very religious. They were all brought up religiously. Not Paul's father's way as I told you. He was a, he was of a reformed, oh my he was bounced up and shaking his fist at me. He heard me say reformed. But I consider it that the friend service in Sweden was a great reformation even on top of Luther's. And Luther by the way was a.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: What kind of effect that had whether you were from the North or South on the political beliefs of the people.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: Well it had some. I don't think that after they got out to American Ridge and they had to dig hard for a living. I don't, I think it began to mean less and less to them. Than what it meant when they were back close to the war. You see they didn't come out here until well after the Civil War.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: And yet.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: Some of the gold from the Pierce Mining District I have somewhere in my notes. I can't say this for a fact now. But somewhere in my notes. I don't know whether these notes are. But some notes that I have had a little in about some of that gold was taken out and shipped to the war. And some of it went to the Confederacy. As they go. But whether it was minted and I didn't trace that. I don't remember what that was. I know I didn't do the tracing of how much it was or whether it was minted or not. I don't know because by that time the country was getting so torn up that the regular system of minting the money wasn't very well known. But I do know that that was said. That here was this note that actually even though the country wasn't settled up out here there was some gold going in. Of course the California gold I've taken. A lot of that was taken into the Civil War. But 1861 to 63 there. Pierce discovered gold at Orifino in 63. Orifino was named by him. Of course gold I find. Only gold.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: Gold I find.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: And I don't know whether I got that into Blake's head or not when he was studying. But I think so though. But Orifino. He's so fond of Orifino.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well listen did it tend to shape would you be a Democrat? More likely to be a Democrat from the South than a Republican from the North?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: Yes that was said on American Age. I've heard the old timers say that. Well it was mainly the Democrats. But that wasn't true on American Age. The Democrats didn't all gather on the southern end of the ridge. And the Northerners. The Republicans all gather on the I was going to say the top of American Age. On the northern end of the ridge. But actually they were nearly all Republican up around Bethel. That was the northern Methodist. Southern Methodist on the northern end of the ridge. I get my north and south all mixed up here. You'll have to kind of remember that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But you're saying that they were nearly all Republicans in the northern Methodist Church?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: No that's the southern Methodist. Well that seems unusual since you're saying that there was a southern church. I know it does. But it was as somebody told me and I think it must have been Mrs. Roberts she was mainly the one that she was catching a lot of those things. They were Republicans of course. And they came from the north. But they were Republicans. But they didn't belong to the church either. That is Mr. Al Roberts didn't. But his brother was a regular. Oh my, yes he was a worth -growing Methodist.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What do you remember about politics from the ridge country and around Troy when you were young?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: I don't remember very much. Al Roberts, it was local politics. I remember quite a little bit about local politics. They were very much interested in local politics. And at that time Moscow, although it was a logical center, you see we didn't have an organization where by we had a central area like Moscow in those days. And the politics of the county were spread all over the county. And consequently it was not until I was coming up to high school age practically when they began to get more. And it was after the university came to Moscow that it became a center. Now I have that timing put out in some of my notebooks.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you get it?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: I don't remember saying that it was the coming of the university that did Moscow. Oh I probably didn't say it but it's implied. Of course I by reading my notes know that the implication was there. That's what I said about a lot of the things in my... I'd almost have to explain a lot of them. Well that's what we were doing. Uh huh. That's it. I would understand the implication.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Okay, what is the implication then?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: How... Well naturally when the university came there were more and more of the young men and women were coming up to the university and getting ideas, taking political science and learning about it and thinking that they should have people that knew a little bit more. And a boy from our age down there, American age, George Davidson, I remember I think he was maybe I don't... wouldn't say he was the first boy to graduate. He was the first one that I knew of to graduate who lived on American age, southern end. Well his father was a Republican, Joe Davidson was a Republican. But he'd come into the country, not he wasn't... he wasn't... I don't think Joe Davidson was either northern or southern in views because he'd come from... oh he was of Scottish extraction I believe and had come by way of Nova Scotia to this country and had lived in Nova Scotia and he came here and as far... I don't know about his... his political affiliations. I didn't know the older Davidsons very well. I knew them but I never talked with them about it. But he was a very good friend of Mr. Roberts and I'm quite sure they were... were Republicans. Well George went to school and I think he was the first boy to graduate from college and well my goodness the farmers all around, including my father, said well it was possible to send your kid off to school and he'd get so much education he'd be an educated fool and that's what George was, he was an educated fool. Come out there and telling them how to farm and telling them how to do this and how to do that and oh yes he knew all about politics and everything else. Well I never heard George talk politics. I never was around. I never knew George until I guess after he graduated the university and he got to going with somebody that I liked awful well and I saw him a few times but they weren't talking politics. I remember that I met them and they were on their way to be married and Fern Carlton and she was my ideal and I wanted to... when I grew up I was going to be like Fern, I was going to teach school like Fern and I met them coming in as rubber tired buggy and oh George did sit up and he did think he was somebody. Anyway I remember that I agreed with everybody around the country because he... but I thought he was too. I sort of agreed with George, he was somebody and sort of made me believe that, you know, the way he looked and most other people didn't. However, I liked George afterward.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it going to college that gave him these heirs?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: I wouldn't know but I suppose so. I never knew him before so I suppose so. His brothers didn't go to college but they went farther than the eighth grade. They went to Kendrick and took all that Kendrick had at that time in the way of high school. It wasn't a full high school I don't think. They were a family who knew quite a little more than just an ordinary rural family. They were... they believed in knowing something but they didn't enter politics at any time. When you talk about local politics you're saying you remember local politics.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What kinds of local issues were there?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: Oh they didn't have issues and talk issues. I didn't learn that until I got in high school, a long time afterward, about issues. I didn't call them issues. I didn't know what they were but they talked about it. They called it talking politics. They talked about what man did this and what man did that and whether to change the road from the southern end of the county to some place where to connect a lot of places. That seemed to be the idea and well they would have to contend with some rich farmer who would try to get the road over his way and that wouldn't benefit these smaller settlers but they all sort of tended toward leaning toward the rich because he could do more for them and give more time to the road to make the road and things of that sort. Now those were the things that they were discussing and then of course in the matter of politics what the men talked about and what the women talked about. The women would always say oh the men let them go out in the other room or go out on the porch where they just want to talk politics and they called a lot of things politics that had no politics in it as I can look back and know but I liked the men's talk a lot better than I did the women. The women talked about the chickens and what kind of chickens they thought were the best and how many eggs they got from their chickens and how much money they got for their eggs during the winter and how to make butter and all that stuff and then how to take care of the babies. And some of them were wise enough to get a magazine that had some late lessons in and they discussed them. Well that was their politics, the babies and chickens and early gardens and all that. And when the men met with any of the women came in they did start talking to them. To my father he was a gardener and I tell you what a professional gardener, best gardener on American marriage I'll swear to that. He was and they asked him and he was an authority on it and still it wasn't from book learning but he was a natural observer and I wish I had his eye for observing everything. And of course in the process as he had grown up in Michigan his father before him and his mother they'd been great believers in the stars and the moon and so on so he planted his potatoes in the moon.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So they'd come by, what you doing Charlie?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: Mighty cold day for me to be planting those potatoes, they won't come up. Planting them in the moon I suppose. Yeah I'm planting them in the moon I would say, had a certain moon, plant them in. Well they won't come up, I'll plant my potatoes about a month later and I'll have better potatoes than you. Okay Grandpa would say, we'll find out and my boys will swear to it that Grandpa's always had the garden and the women would all say that. Charlie will have the best garden, you all know that. So you go home and get the garden ready and they did. They didn't plant in the moon, they planted after Charlie. All the good gardeners wanted to have good gardens, try to plant them when Papa did. And so when the men got together they would talk about, to the women, because the women had an awful lot more to do with gardening than the men did in most cases, not in our case. Grandpa did it and then we children had the garden. Oh I hated it when I was a kid, but I did learn it and learned it right and right now I'd rather garden than do housework. So the men talked about that and they did talk about the kinds of chickens that they thought laid the best. And Papa was always out for hints on building chicken houses and things like that. Well now that was their politics and that's what they talked. But when the men were by themselves they talked politics. When the men were by themselves they talked what the women called politics. But as I say their politics included a lot of things. They did include the kind of wheat.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were they going to change to this new kind of wheat?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: Well now they understood that up at the university they were trying to create another kind of wheat. Well that was their job. Some men would explain to the farmers that didn't know because a lot of the farmers didn't know. Some of the more learned ones would say that that was their research and that was right, that they were searching it out. But that was how we got the university. As Brigham, who was one of the main ones over here at Genesee, a county commissioner from Genesee and Mitchum from Kendrick, that got the university and largely through getting it through the Morrill Act, which created the Agricultural School at the university. That is also the reason why Boise has not been able to cart the university away from Moscow to Boise as they've tried to do many times and thought they'd do. They thought they'd steal the law department. They finally did get Boise College and then kept on adding more colleges until practically they had a university down there. But they couldn't kill the University of Idaho because they had the Morrill Act up here and so many things had been created through the Morrill Act at the university. So that in 1909 when Teddy Roosevelt came here to speak in front of the university, he spoke on a pile of sacks, I think I've written it down someplace or have it in the paper, on sacks of wheat that were around in front of Morrill Hall. And that was the reason. It was a big thing.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did the Morrill Act specify?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: Well Morrill was an ag man and he left it to an ag college for the purposes of research and developing the country around where it was built. And here was a fertile field up here in the Palouse country, which is of course one of the fertile spots of Idaho, but it isn't so big. It isn't anywhere near as big as South Idaho. But of course at that time South Idaho was a waste of desert land. And the first crop of people that were to come in and get us started and dig the ditches after a while, after everybody had gone broke and gotten out of there because their windmills wouldn't work, they didn't have enough wind to run them to get water into their ditches and they didn't know enough about irrigation. And these people up here were speculating if those fellas ever get irrigation down there. Well there were two schools of thought as I remember that some of the people would say eh, you'll never be able to get the water turned onto the land down there in that desert to make anything grow. And then the others, oh you'd just be surprised, that'll make that a fertile garden and someday it will be. Well why don't you go down there and take a claim and you can get it almost for nothing. Well they didn't want to be among those first ones. They felt awful sorry for those people that were in there building up. And of course they did leave and there were well improved farms people that spent a lot of money on there when they left. Well you see, that's what they did. I don't know, oh I haven't read about the Morrill Act for a long time. I used to know that part.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think, that makes me wonder, do you think that the farmers that were, that did come to this country came partly because, and stayed partly because it was more like the old kind of farming that they knew how to do? As compared to this irrigated, newfangled farm?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: Oh yes, that's what settled this country, sure. They didn't want to come there. That took a lot of, it took a lot of legislation there. And men that worked on that, finally the Cary Act went through from, and that's, they began settling the southern Idaho and Wyoming and the places where they could have, and sections of Montana. And they got in under the Cary Act. And the Cary Act, now I'm not conversant enough with the Cary Act to start talking to you about it, but at any rate it assisted with getting irrigation into the country. And getting the streams ready for and prepared for it. And of course it had, they had to get down below the source of supply, naturally, to get the water directed in that direction and then big irrigation ditches made and they certainly did dig them in those days. And there's work for engineers, plenty of work for that. And some of these long -sighted men were saying, with this university here, the boys can get trained, we'll get engineers, we have to have surveyors, and this land's going to have to be surveyed. And these, and the irrigation ditches will have to be taken care of. The plants will have to be run and all that sort of thing. And these men were talking about that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What's going to happen to the country?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: The local people. Local people. I listened to that. It's going to be the country around here. Sure.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And how's it going to work?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: Oh yes, and that's what they're going to do down there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, they weren't thinking about irrigation for up here, were they?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: Oh no.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: It was a nice little town, wasn't it?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: Although...

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: ...to the country around here.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How was it?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: They didn't know, but they speculated. Some thought this was going to be a wonderful country, just had to have more development, and it did. And then, of course, marketing was a problem. Lee Carlton was a man. He had a fruit dryer down the lower end of American Ridge, southern end of American Ridge, at least the south of us. We had a vantage point right in the middle of the ridge between Troy and Kenry. Now, for instance, my father had worked for Martin Thomas, who lived on the very end of American Ridge, southern end, and had a fertile spot down there, Mercy, had gotten a homestead and bought out to us the other pre -emptions to come in with a little money. And a man who came in with a little money could make a lot of money. He didn't have the source of supply that the J .P. Ballmer did in the early days. Later on, he did. He became the president of the bank down at Kendrick, and he said whether the local people were going to be able to borrow money or not, and of course, I guess he borrowed on his own. I don't know about that. I never heard enough to know. But they all finally had to get a bank, so they'd have the control of some money that kept building and building bigger and bigger farms and getting more and more money. So he was a very wealthy man, and they talked about him and how he was building, and the men did. And I heard all this. Men were the ones that were doing the things.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They were the ones that were deciding whether they were going to have big farms or little farms, and what in the world are the few settings of eggs? What difference did that make, you know?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: And I was never interested in that. And the men didn't talk about things I was interested in all the time, but sometimes they did. I liked them to get into constitutional arguments and all that sort of thing.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did they?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: No. No. Nobody did.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: Oh, once in a while somebody would bring up constitutional law, and they talked about which lawyer was the best. I remember Suppiger was a lawyer in Moscow.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Now, what?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: Farmers ought to have a lawyer that... A lawyer ought to be here so that farmers had to go to law about anything. He'd be sympathetic.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, what man do you think would be the most sympathetic?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: Well, that fella didn't know anything. Some fella had run in with this lawyer or that, and they got some erroneous ideas about who that lawyer should be. And I remember that they were very strong for Bertonell French. They hadn't looked into Bertonell French's background. I know that, but I know it from my years of learning afterward. But as I recall it then, well, it was good to get Bertonell in as a congressman because he told them he'd work for them and so on. He never worked for anybody but himself. He only worked for Bertonell French. And he was too narrow -minded and too empty -minded to know very much about what to do. He just went out. He graduated the University of Idaho, and he had worked for his way through school. He was to be admired for that. And I had his brother, younger brother, a Carl in French, for a teacher one year. And he was an excellent teacher. And I believe he knew more in one minute than Bertonell did in a week.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, would people realize that Bertonell French wasn't really doing anything for them after he got in and didn't do much?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: Oh, I think a lot of people did, and all the Democrats did, of course. I had my eyes open, and I was a Democrat long before I ever dared say that.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: Well, did the people... did people believe that...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: what was their idea of what politics was about? What did they think?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: They didn't know politics.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How could they talk real politics?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: They didn't know real politics. But they did talk about personalities a lot who represented politics. And once in a while, they knew a little more. Now, Mr. Roberts, I loved him. Mr. and Mrs. Roberts, I loved them. They were wonderful people. And I'm not saying one thing against them, and I wouldn't write down a thing against Al Roberts for anything in this world. But like him talking about Frank May, who had spent all his life collecting mortgages. Well, he tried to collect hunting trophies. And he spent days and days trekking across the wilderness. He was in the Settleway Forest long before any other white men had gone up through there. He met and made friends with the Indians. He was one of the best ambassadors of goodwill among people. And he... Al Roberts didn't have any, any, any ambitions about race. If a Negro had come to his house, he would have taken him in and kept him overnight. He hadn't known many. They didn't travel up into Pennsylvania and Ohio. So he hadn't known many Negroes. And I never heard his philosophy about Negroes, except that I know his style. And I do know that old Joe Wells, he would see, meet him on the street in Troy when he came down there once in a while. And I'll bet he never had a higher handshake than he had from Al Roberts. And he stayed at Joe Wells' house up in Derry when he was around with the assessing and told about that. That he'd stayed overnight there. And old Joe Wells and his family, how great they were. And they got out there, musical instruments. I don't know what they'd played. I don't know that. Because I didn't know those Negroes. For goodness sakes, I was kept in home. I stayed home. And my mother was very particular about me. And when I went to town, I had to stay under their supervision very close. And when I got to going with the neighbors, well, there weren't any Negroes around town there. But they had to play musical instruments and had a good time. Well, had a good time and sang.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: But you're about to say about Al Roberts' politics, politically.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: That's what you started with. He was a Republican. And that's what he knew. And that was Abraham Lincoln politics. It was Abraham Lincoln who freed the slaves. Actually, actually, they were freed by a lot of Southerners, too. Many Southerners had helped. And as far as that was concerned. But then Abraham Lincoln had said, as a young man, that if he ever got a chance to strike a blow at slavery, he would. And he did. And he had that. And he actually believed he was doing this for the freeing of slaves. And he did go through that. But right on the...when you know history, you know that right along with it, you know, the Republican tariff men were running up the high tariffs to protect their industries here at home. Here at home, it didn't amount to very much. And they were doing a lot of things. And, of course, the Southerners didn't now, altogether. Because they were raising cotton and they wanted a good price for their cotton. And they didn't want a high tariff on it. And they didn't want to have to have it so that they had to pay the Northerners a big price to get their cotton manufactured here at home. So the Southerners weren't in favor of a high tariff. But believe me, the Northern Republicans were. And they more or less fooled Abraham Lincoln. But, you know, he hadn't had a chance to have an education. And he couldn't possibly have known everything. When I first learned that he had some weak spots, I thought that was too bad because I'd been taught to. Abraham Lincoln was a hero. And I'd heard people say, well, if they were going to worship a man, they believed that every man had to have somebody to worship. And I heard people talking about that. Well, I listened to all these things and took them in. I did not believe. I'm not born to be a believer of everything I hear. But that was my good fortune. That is, I consider my good fortune. Maybe not. Maybe you should believe. They said if you're going to believe in somebody... But if you're going to worship, why, you could just as well worship a man. They were more or less atheistic. Atheistic until they came to die. And they were talking about Abraham Lincoln as being... Well, I heard different people say, or I heard them say, I heard it said, let me say it that way, I don't know for sure who said it. But that if they were going to worship a man in the United States, they'd worship Abraham Lincoln because he came in the interest of being a god, a man who stood head and shoulders above every other man. Well, now Abraham Lincoln didn't really stand head and shoulders above other men. He did make a great president and certainly did wonderfully well for his background. And I'm willing to honor him.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: Yes.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: I pay great honor to a man like Abraham Lincoln who could come out of the background like that and do as well as he did and have the lofty ideals that he had. But at the same time, he was a mere man and he had the faults of a man. He had human failings and they were bad ones. Very bad ones. Big ones.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: And George Washington was far from being a man to worship. Like after the Revolutionary War, I know that I've heard different ones say, historians talking about it, well it looks as though, it appears as though people were in the mood to worship a man like Washington afterward. A leader, a great leader at a time like that gets a lot more acclaim than what he deserves and probably a great deal more than what he wanted. I'm sure Abraham Lincoln didn't ask for it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well do you think that Lincoln was the most popular politician on the ridge?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: Of course he was dead by then. Oh no, by then he was a dead hero.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Who were some of the other personalities that you say that they talked personalities? Who were some of the others that they talked about?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: Well, I talked personalities when I said they talked personalities. I was talking about personalities of the time being, of their own, among their people.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, was Lafayette King good enough to be elected county commissioner again? Was Manfred Harland a good county commissioner? Good enough to be elected again? Or should they get somebody else in? What would he favor?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: And there had been Cattlemen's Association and then some people were strong for the law and other people were wondering about those vigilante committees. We weren't very far at that time from the vigilante committee days when people used to get out and have, they had sort of a secret committee of vigilantes that if there was any trouble in the neighborhood the vigilantes got together and they said they had hangings. I knew a man, a little older than I, named Raymond Harland and he told me that he never did get into it, never did find out, but that his father, and that was Manfred Harland, he was the neighbor on the other side of us, belonged to, he said he learned after he got to going to school one thing or another it was a vigilante committee and he asked his father and his father never told him, he just laughed and joked at him and gave him jokes. But Raymond says he belonged to that and said because I went and listened one night and they met in our greenery and he said and they talked about there were some horse thieves in the neighborhood and what should they do with them and they had caught them and they all knew where they were. Raymond said he never found out and he said he and another kid told who the kid was and I forgot who it was, but I didn't dare put that in my notes either. They'd make a noise so that their fathers would hear them and, but they got by and they listened to this meeting they had under that tree, big old tree. And uh, oh they heard them discussing and they decided they were going to hang that man. But uh, he didn't know whether they did or not, but they, he didn't know that, but he thought that they did and he thought they hung them there on that tree and he said after that, they, the children, the kids, oh they were going to be pretty big kids, called out the hanging tree as far as they were concerned, that was the hanging tree. They'd listen to this meeting, but his father went away that night, that next night, but he saw to it that the mother had those kids locked in so Raymond didn't get out. So he didn't get to go there, but they would have gone and seen whether they had the hanging or not, but he was pretty sure that everybody was pretty solemn about that. That was our thieves. They caught them with their horses, but now I don't know those particulars.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You said the Cattlemen's Association, this wasn't cattle country, wasn't it?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: No, I said Cattlemen's Association because I am very well acquainted with Idaho history. Idaho history. And there were Cattlemen's Associations. And they were vigilante committees. And the vigilante committees were all over the state. And you read an Idaho history and you'll find that out. I know about it, I mean I've heard of it. Well, I'm sure you would have in the work that you're doing.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: If you don't believe it, now I was going to say to you that the reason, you know, a lot of people have said to me, why in the world didn't you, they thought I was lazy, and I got lazy after I got sick, but why didn't I get all this material put into a book?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: And I did sort of regret it at one time, and then when I see some of these people coming out with things that are much less, but I don't know, as I say, I was interested in the human interest part of it. And I wasn't so interested in the history part. But if you think there's not Idaho history written, all you need to do is look at one of my bibliographies. Oh yeah. And I, why I did research, my goodness sakes, and read on all this history.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I've got all this, I don't know whether you noticed that or not, on Bora?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: Didn't see that particularly.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: Oh, well here's all this material.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you hear anything else about vigilante stuff around?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: Oh, not around the effects of it, this county, no I didn't.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: But, people knew that it was, but they were pretty close mouthed about anybody that might have belonged. Now Raymond, in talking to me about his father, he didn't expect me to go blab that or put it in any book.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: You know, that even.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: And that was carefully guarded, that secret was carefully guarded, and so, as Raymond said, you know they never told anything that you could get hold of. But, I know that that man's wife didn't know anything about it. She didn't bother about any of his meetings.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: Do you think that it had anything to do with the, what you mentioned, one of the quotes in the newspaper articles that was in your manuscript, that the people in the country were holding the land for their friends. And I wondered if it had, I know that a lot of the vigilante stuff in some areas had to do with land possession.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Who would be allowed to take the land?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: If you were holding the land, you wanted somebody to have it, not just anybody that came along.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that that was at all a factor? It was involved at all?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: I'm just curious, I don't know if that would have anything to do with vigilantes or not. No, it didn't have anything to do with vigilantes. But, in some cases, I expect it was.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: For example, you found a lot of good land, even as I said in my notes, that, why were they coming out here?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: Well, they'd heard from these Brillharts who'd come ahead. They rode back to Kansas and told their neighbors, get up so and so and so and so, well, Al Roberts happened to be one of them. And come on out and there's just, the land is fertile here and it's here for the taking. But you have to get here before the others do because there's going to be a lot of people come in. And they knew, first come, first served. Now, of course, there were squatters. So they might have, they might go over and build a shack or something and let one of their older kids live in it or something of that sort to fool people, to make it, to indicate that they had that land too for a little while, to stall off people coming in and taking it. I've heard of them doing that, but I don't know of anybody who did it, really.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: It doesn't really matter to me, names or anything. No. I'm just curious about the kind of tactics that people would use.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that they would try to actively discourage people who came out looking and say, oh, all the land is taken and that's it?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: Oh, yes. If they didn't want them there, they did. You bet they did.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: They discouraged them and they looked on with disfavor, people that came in. Yes, I'm sure they would do that. I'm very sure that they did that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you know, would you say though, that many of the people that came in were unknown to everybody when they came in and yet came in and established themselves and were wholly accepted? Or do you think that most of the people that came out were in some way related to people that were older?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: Yes, they had some kind of an inkling that here was a place for them to go. And it was only later when the old real estate companies began to send out men and locators, they called them, yes, and advertise that they'd locate you on a claim if you came there. Then people came out and strays would go in and try to get located. And if they didn't suit the neighborhood, they didn't have a nice time. That's true of all pioneer communities, I think. But I don't know of anybody being run out and there were all kinds of people that came in. Now, for instance, Frank May came in from Oregon and I don't think he had any people here, but he did know some people that lived out close to Moscow. Well, they said there's land on claim down there. And so, yes, they'd get some of these agents, oh, you know how those land sharks are, they were anxious to make money without doing anything. And they would go out and locate you on some land that's left and they'd keep maps of the country.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would you say that most of the people that came out were related to somebody who was there?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: Most of them were, on American Ridge were, yes. But they came out with a whole bunch of them came together. Now, they might have paid some locator, they might have paid some real estate man to give them an idea or maybe a map of a region. But they didn't have them go and locate them. And then they went out and if it was surveyed land, they'd get maps from the land office. And it was surveyed up in here at that time. Now, that was the reason given for the Russians who camped here at that time. It wasn't, they didn't stay here because they didn't want, they were told they didn't drive them out. But they would be squatters on property because it was not surveyed. And so when they went to the land office to try to find out, they couldn't get clear title, they knew that because it was not surveyed. But you could squat on it and then take your chances on being able to get it when it came time, when it was surveyed.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you know at what point roughly the American Ridge Country was closed because it was all homesteaded and after that it was only sold?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: If a person wanted to buy, he'd be buying from a homesteader.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you know how quickly all the homestead land was actually taken there?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: Oh, I don't know how fast it was taken.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: I was born in 1896 and I couldn't be expected to know very much but 1900 could I. It's not about the land. I didn't give a hoot at that time. Well, I suppose that by the time you get to people who were buying land, then the land that was available was probably all taken. Well, there was no more land available for homesteads. And my father, there was no land available for a homestead for him. And he bought that place and all the, let's see, he bought 40 acres from Mr. Roberts.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: Oh, I got so interested in this stuff the other night that I stayed up reading it until 4 o 'clock. I got reanimated. I told Mavis, I said, I'm terribly interested in this stuff. And regardless of my eyes, of course I had to stop to read these times, bathe my eyes. It was a steady reading. And I looked at the clock and it was 4 o 'clock. Why, I haven't done that since I was in high school studying. Absolutely. But I found out about Poppa in there, about him buying 40 acres off Val Roberts' homestead. And then I said, of course I didn't make myself particularly popular in my family by saying it's poetic justice. Because Mr. Roberts had out of the kindness of his heart, sold off 40 acres of that land to pay the debts for men who had gone bankrupt. And for whom he had cosigned their notes at the banks and then of course they couldn't pay. And Al Roberts had to pay because he was a cosigner and the only way he could raise money was to sell some of his homestead. And he sold 40 acres.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When was this about?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: When did you... Oh, well it's in here, what year it is, but I know what time it was about. It was right close to the beginning of the century. It's a way back there. And it sent this down. But I don't carry all those dates in my mind.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you know how your husband became a Democrat?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: Oh, with his parents.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: Very liberal. His father in fact was considered a socialist with a socialism. But down here where they didn't have a socialist party where they acted as Democrats. I think my husband was... Well, he had leanings toward that line. They were pretty strong that way. They were known in the old country as liberals.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: Yes, he was always a Democrat. He never was anything else. He didn't come up in a neighborhood where they were Republican or where they learned like my friends were Republicans. But however, on the other side of us lived Manfred Harlan and Manfred Harlan was a Democrat. And I didn't have to get very old to know that Manfred Harlan... Now for heaven's sake don't ever let this get into my book, into any notes. I wouldn't say that they're descendants of those people living. But it didn't take me forever to learn that Manfred Harlan was the smarter of the two men. And that what he said, he was more of a leader, and that what he said about the qualities of the men they were putting in as county commissioners especially, who had to do with the doings in the county. They decided a lot of things. They had to... I don't know what all they did do, but they had to go and ride out different trails for roads. They saw to it. And they helped set the taxes, what the taxes were going to be. They found where the schools were going to be, if the school was big enough or too big. Or if they had to have them close enough together that the children didn't have to walk too far to get to school.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: And they had to take care of the poor in the county. They had to look out for them. Oh, there were lots of things that had to be done. And they needed men to do them. So, some of the talk went on around the porch when I was curled around the post of the Roberts Planners house. I'd be curled around that post. And Mr.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Roberts used to wind up by striking out with a glove or a handkerchief or a newspaper at me and saying, What do you think about it, Willa?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: And I always had an answer. I learned that that was a smart thing to do. And that I also learned how to tone my answers. I wasn't to be a flip. I'd have gotten spanked if I had been flippant. Well, that never occurred to me to be that way.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you remember what kind of answers you might give?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: No, I don't remember the answers. But I know they said I always answered. And I lived to learn that by being careful and by knowing what I was talking about, that I gained the respect of people around me.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: Did he have pretty strong ideas of Mr.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Harland about the difference between the good politicians and the bad ones?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: Mr. Harland. I bet he had strong ideas. He didn't always air them completely out. He kept his neighborhood in a pretty good spirit.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And I told him about the political speeches, didn't I?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: In your book.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: He goes there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you remember any of those specific, I mean, of a guy coming out and speaking?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: Oh, goodness, yes. I should say I went to him. The women were invited out, of course. But no women went except Ada Harland, who was ten years older than I. She didn't want to be the only girl there, so she'd get me to go. And I was delighted to go. And of course if Ada hadn't been going, I wouldn't have gotten to go alone. And Papa thought it was kind of funny because I would tell it to him. I remembered much better than Papa said. And he'd ask me if I remembered what so -and -so said. I remember Meriwether Lewis, M .H. Lewis. Of course the Republicans were more favored by Papa and Mr. Roberts. And it was easier for me to get to go. But I went to the Democrats, too.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: When they came.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: I wasn't old enough. When I went to any of those, I was not old enough to discern the differences or to be critical of the differences. But I do remember that Meriwether Lewis. He was running for state representative. And they stayed all night at Mr. Roberts's. Mrs. Roberts wasn't home. And of course he took in all the Republicans. They all stayed. They made beds for him at Mr. Roberts's. And they went down there. But he came up and asked my mother to be sure they were just across the road. She'd give them breakfast, supper and breakfast. And accordingly they did. They were there for that. And of course they complimented her on everything, including my baby sister. And she was, oh I don't know, she wasn't walking. And she's a great big heavy baby. And she became a very good looking young woman. Far better looking than I. But nevertheless, she was a homely little Dickens at that time. And so he bragged on her. And so mama was smiling and taking it all in. And I got close to her and pulled her dress. You know, I just kept pulling her dress. Now I wasn't very tall then. Because I kept pulling at her dress. And then I got around and then I looked up at her and frowned. And she told me, oh go on and do something. She didn't want me there. She ignored what I was trying to do for her. And I remember that after the breakfast, this was after they'd eaten breakfast. He was standing there dishing out this ladder egg. And he only got into the, I remember that she got into the syrup pitcher. And put syrup out. I suppose that we'd had hotcakes for breakfast. I don't remember what we had for breakfast. But it sounds about to me as if we'd had hotcakes. And she had syrup all over her. And she was dirty. Her dress was dirty.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: And she just looked awful.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And well he said, and mom said, oh my goodness what in the world have you been into? And then she said to me, can't you clean the baby's face off?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: Well of course I'm nine years, almost nine years older than she was. So I was between nine and ten. I wasn't so little. I knew enough to try to hide from him that I was trying to attract my mother's attention.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: I did. I took her and got away from there. I was glad to get her away and wash her face. And she bawled like a fright. I remember that. Now when I think of it, I remember her crying sard. Of course I don't suppose I was very clever about doing it either. But anyway, she got down and I don't believe she walked. I think she crawled back to my mother. Well, Mr. Lewis was telling her that they would have so many things they were going to work for the farmers. And he said, envision better homes and all this sort of thing. And I already knew that Ford Robbins could build telephones. And he came from Chicago and I was all in favor waiting on Fort and his cohorts. So in other words, this guy was not...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: he was flattering?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: Yes, and he was false. I knew it. I said, I wouldn't vote for that man. I wouldn't vote for that man. He's no good.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Papa said, why not?

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: That makes you say that. That was after Papa got in. And I told him what she did. And he looked at me and I said, Mama took it all in. And I said, she promised him she'd vote for him. Oh, she said, I didn't either. My mother was kind of ashamed of it afterwards too because Papa said to me, he knew I was right. He said, I don't believe these guys go around and do politics. I don't believe them at all. But I do remember that this fellow that's with him, I can't remember his name now, but I did know him. And Papa knew him. And he said, oh, he couldn't expect anything out of him. But he said he's just a bag of wind like his old dad was. But I can't remember his name. But anyway, I remember from their speech. I don't remember much from their speeches.

speaker: Willa Cummings Carlson, Mavis Lee Utley or Helen Johnson
words: Do you think that in office that some of them were...

speaker: Sam Schrager
